stem: Let \( f(x) = [[A]]x^{2} + ([[B]])x + ([[C]]) \), which is continuous on \( [[[p]], [[q]]] \) and differentiable on \( ([[p]], [[q]]) \). The Mean Value Theorem guarantees a point \( c \in ([[p]], [[q]]) \) with \( f'(c) = \dfrac{f([[q]])-f([[p]])}{[[q]]-[[p]]} \). What is \( c \)?
solution: Since \( f \) is quadratic, \( f'(x) = 2([[A]])x+([[B]]) \) is linear, so \( f'(x) \) equals the average slope exactly once. The average rate of change is \( \dfrac{f([[q]])-f([[p]])}{[[q]]-[[p]]} = [[A]]([[p]]+[[q]])+[[B]] \); setting \( 2[[A]]c+[[B]] \) equal to this and solving gives \( c = \dfrac{[[p]]+[[q]]}{2} = [[answer]] \), the midpoint of the interval.
distractor_rationales: This reports the left endpoint of the interval instead of the guaranteed interior point.
This reports the right endpoint of the interval instead of the guaranteed interior point.
This is the midpoint shifted one unit too high.
This is the midpoint shifted one unit too low.
This is off by two from the correct value of \( c \).
This is off by two from the correct value of \( c \).

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[A]]x^{3} + ([[B]])x^{2} + ([[C]])x + ([[D]]) \) on \( [[[p]], [[q]]] \). By the Mean Value Theorem there is some \( c \in ([[p]], [[q]]) \) with \( f'(c) \) equal to the average rate of change of \( f \) over \( [[[p]], [[q]]] \). What is that guaranteed value of \( f'(c) \)?
solution: The average rate of change is \( \dfrac{f([[q]])-f([[p]])}{[[q]]-[[p]]} \). Expanding \( f([[q]])-f([[p]]) \) and dividing by \( [[q]]-[[p]] \) gives \( [[A]](p^{2}+pq+q^{2}) + [[B]](p+q) + [[C]] = [[answer]] \), which the MVT guarantees equals \( f'(c) \) for some interior \( c \).
distractor_rationales: This evaluates \( f'(p) \), the slope at the left endpoint, not the average rate of change.
This evaluates \( f'(q) \), the slope at the right endpoint, not the average rate of change.
This drops the cross term \( pq \) from the cubic's expansion.
This mistakenly expands \( (p+q)^{2} \) instead of \( p^{2}+pq+q^{2} \).
This is off by three from the correct value.
This is off by three from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[A]]\sin(x) + ([[B]])x \) on \( [0, [[n]]\pi] \). By the Mean Value Theorem there is some \( c \in (0, [[n]]\pi) \) with \( f'(c) \) equal to the average rate of change of \( f \) over \( [0, [[n]]\pi] \). What is that guaranteed value of \( f'(c) \)?
solution: Since \( \sin([[n]]\pi) = 0 = \sin(0) \), the average rate of change is \( \dfrac{([[A]]\cdot 0+[[B]]\cdot [[n]]\pi) - ([[A]]\cdot 0+[[B]]\cdot 0)}{[[n]]\pi} = [[B]] = [[answer]] \), which the MVT guarantees equals \( f'(c) = [[A]]\cos(c)+[[B]] \) for some interior \( c \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the sine coefficient to \( B \) instead of recognizing that the sine terms cancel at multiples of \( \pi \).
This mistakenly evaluates the derivative formula \( f'(0) \) instead of the average rate of change.
This has the wrong sign on the sine-coefficient contribution.
This incorrectly divides the linear coefficient by \( n \).
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
